raw_response:
{
    "internal_reasoning": "This artwork, identified as 'No. 31 (Yellow Stripe)' by Mark Rothko and dated to 1958, represents a quintessential example of the artist's mature 'Classic' period. Rothko is a titan of the Abstract Expressionist movement, and his works from the mid-to-late 1950s are considered the pinnacle of his artistic achievement, commanding the highest prices in the post-war market. \n\nVisually, the painting exhibits Rothko's signature 'multiform' composition: large, soft-edged rectangular fields of color floating against a stained ground. The specific arrangement here—a luminous peach/orange upper rectangle, a deep crimson/maroon lower rectangle, and a vibrant yellow ground that forms a distinct horizontal 'stripe' or separator—is highly characteristic of his exploration of color relationships. The technique involves thin washes of oil paint (glazing) applied in multiple layers, creating a sense of inner light and depth that is palpable even in reproduction. The edges are fuzzy and blurred (sfumato), allowing the colors to 'breathe' and vibrate against one another, a technique Rothko perfected to evoke emotional and spiritual responses.\n\nThe year 1958 is particularly significant. It places the work at the height of Rothko's fame, just as he was beginning the famous Seagram Murals commission. While the Seagram works are darker and more somber, this piece retains the luminous, warm palette that is most coveted by private collectors. The combination of yellow, orange, and red is historically one of Rothko's most successful and valuable color symphonies (e.g., *Orange, Red, Yellow* sold for $86.9 million in 2012; *No. 10* sold for $81.9 million in 2015).\n\nRegarding market comparables, works from this specific period (1957-1960) in this classic style regularly achieve prices between $40 million and $60 million at major auctions (Christie's, Sotheby's). While the market has seen some fluctuation since the 2014-2015 peaks, top-tier Rothkos remain 'blue-chip' assets that are largely immune to downturns. The condition appears pristine in the image, with no visible cracking, fading, or restoration, which is critical for a work of this age. Assuming a solid provenance (as implied by the prompt's condition of 'legally available'), this work would be a headline lot in any major evening sale.\n\nMy valuation balances the record-breaking sales of similar luminous 1950s Rothkos against a slightly more conservative current market estimate for a work that, while magnificent, may not have the specific celebrity provenance of the $80m+ records. A fair market value in the low-to-mid $50 millions is realistic.",
    "valuation_usd": 52000000,
    "confidence": 0.85,
    "key_factors": [
        "Artist Significance (Mark Rothko, Abstract Expressionism)",
        "Period (1958 - Peak 'Classic' Style)",
        "Color Palette (Luminous warm tones: Yellow, Orange, Red)",
        "Condition (Appears pristine, no visible damage)",
        "Market Comparables (Recent sales of similar 1950s works range $40m-$80m)"
    ]
}

parsed.internal_reasoning:
This artwork, identified as 'No. 31 (Yellow Stripe)' by Mark Rothko and dated to 1958, represents a quintessential example of the artist's mature 'Classic' period. Rothko is a titan of the Abstract Expressionist movement, and his works from the mid-to-late 1950s are considered the pinnacle of his artistic achievement, commanding the highest prices in the post-war market. 

Visually, the painting exhibits Rothko's signature 'multiform' composition: large, soft-edged rectangular fields of color floating against a stained ground. The specific arrangement here—a luminous peach/orange upper rectangle, a deep crimson/maroon lower rectangle, and a vibrant yellow ground that forms a distinct horizontal 'stripe' or separator—is highly characteristic of his exploration of color relationships. The technique involves thin washes of oil paint (glazing) applied in multiple layers, creating a sense of inner light and depth that is palpable even in reproduction. The edges are fuzzy and blurred (sfumato), allowing the colors to 'breathe' and vibrate against one another, a technique Rothko perfected to evoke emotional and spiritual responses.

The year 1958 is particularly significant. It places the work at the height of Rothko's fame, just as he was beginning the famous Seagram Murals commission. While the Seagram works are darker and more somber, this piece retains the luminous, warm palette that is most coveted by private collectors. The combination of yellow, orange, and red is historically one of Rothko's most successful and valuable color symphonies (e.g., *Orange, Red, Yellow* sold for $86.9 million in 2012; *No. 10* sold for $81.9 million in 2015).

Regarding market comparables, works from this specific period (1957-1960) in this classic style regularly achieve prices between $40 million and $60 million at major auctions (Christie's, Sotheby's). While the market has seen some fluctuation since the 2014-2015 peaks, top-tier Rothkos remain 'blue-chip' assets that are largely immune to downturns. The condition appears pristine in the image, with no visible cracking, fading, or restoration, which is critical for a work of this age. Assuming a solid provenance (as implied by the prompt's condition of 'legally available'), this work would be a headline lot in any major evening sale.

My valuation balances the record-breaking sales of similar luminous 1950s Rothkos against a slightly more conservative current market estimate for a work that, while magnificent, may not have the specific celebrity provenance of the $80m+ records. A fair market value in the low-to-mid $50 millions is realistic.
key_factors: Color Palette (Luminous warm tones: Yellow, Orange, Red)
Market Comparables (Recent sales of similar 1950s works range $40m-$80m)
